nativeRange.habitat: Found in fast-flowing rivers and tributaries where submerged driftwood and rocky substrates are abundant, the Royal Pleco is native to the Orinoco and Rio Negro river systems. It inhabits areas with large quantities of organic wood debris on the riverbed.

decorPreferences: large quantities of driftwood — essential, not optional (hardwoods such as mopani, bogwood; softwoods also accepted)
smooth substrate (fine sand or rounded gravel) to protect ventral surfaces
large flat rocks and slate ledges for resting
robust anchored plants or none (may bulldoze delicate planting)
caves or overhangs for shelter (PVC pipe, clay pot, or stone cave)
low to moderate current to mimic riverine habitat

diet.notes: The Royal Pleco is an obligate xylivore — meaning it derives nutrition from wood itself, a capability shared by very few vertebrates. This is made possible by specialised microbial communities in its hindgut that break down cellulose. Driftwood must be available at all times and will be rasped continuously; without it, the fish will decline. Both hardwoods such as mopani and bogwood and softer woods are accepted. Supplemental feeding should include blanched vegetables such as zucchini, cucumber, sweet potato, and spinach, along with sinking algae wafers and spirulina-based foods, with vegetable matter making up the majority of the supplemental diet. Small amounts of protein — sinking pellets or bloodworm — can be offered occasionally but are not a dietary requirement. The Royal Pleco does not rely on algae in the same way as Ancistrus or Hypostomus species.

goodWith: other large South American fish (Arowanas, large Characins — in very large tanks)
robust large catfish kept in spacious setups with defined territories
large tetras (e.g. Buenos Aires tetra) sharing upper/mid water column
avoidWith: conspecifics (extremely territorial toward other Panaque; serious injury likely in confined space)
other large plecos sharing the same territory
small or delicate fish that may be stressed by size and nocturnal movement
invertebrates (shrimp and snails may be eaten or crushed)
fish requiring pristine low-waste water (Royal Plecos produce heavy bioload)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Panaque nigrolineatus; shows characteristic bold cream-and-brown striped pattern and red eye.
Wikimedia Commons image showing adult specimen with striking dark lines on pale/cream body. Good full-body lateral view.

summary: The Royal Pleco (Panaque nigrolineatus) is one of the very few vertebrates capable of digesting wood, a trait known as xylivory. Reaching 40–50 cm, it requires a tank of at least 500 litres with a continuous supply of driftwood. Its bold cream-and-brown striped pattern and vivid red eyes make it immediately recognizable.
careNotes:
The Royal Pleco (Panaque nigrolineatus, L190) is an exceptional and scientifically remarkable fish — one of the very few vertebrates known to derive nutrition from wood through xylivory, facilitated by specialised microbial hindgut symbionts. This is not merely a preference: driftwood must be present continuously and in substantial quantity, as the fish will rasp it constantly. Without wood, Royal Plecos will decline and die. Hardwoods (mopani, bogwood, ironwood) and softwoods are both accepted; provide multiple large pieces and replace or supplement as they are consumed.

Adult size of 40–50 cm demands a minimum 500 L tank with a long footprint (180 cm+). The Royal Pleco produces an extremely heavy bioload — a consequence of the cellulose-heavy diet generating large quantities of wood-fibre waste — and powerful, oversized filtration (aiming for 8–10× turnover per hour) is essential. Weekly water changes of 30–50% are strongly recommended.

Water should be soft to moderately hard (gH 2–15), slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5), and well-oxygenated. Temperature range is 23–28 °C. The species originates from the Orinoco and Rio Negro basins, where water is often soft and tea-coloured from tannins; adding Indian almond leaves or driftwood to acidify and tint the water is beneficial.

Royal Plecos are highly territorial toward conspecifics and other large plecos; only one specimen should be kept per tank unless the aquarium is very large (1,000 L+) with clearly defined separate territories and duplicate hiding spots. They are largely nocturnal but will emerge during daytime with adequate shelter provided.

The fish is sensitive to copper-based medications and most treatments used for parasites — always research medication compatibility. Maintain stable water chemistry; avoid sudden parameter swings. Supplement with blanched vegetables several times per week. Despite the advanced care requirements, well-maintained Royal Plecos are long-lived (10–20+ years) and display a strikingly beautiful pattern of bold dark lines on a cream to pale-gold body, with vivid red irises.
breedingNotes: Breeding in the home aquarium is extremely rare and considered one of the most difficult achievements in the catfish hobby. In the wild, Panaque nigrolineatus spawns in submerged wood cavities. To attempt captive breeding, a very large species-only setup (1,000 L minimum) with deep wood caves of appropriate dimensions is required. Both parents are reported to guard eggs in cave spawns. The diet of wood cellulose likely plays a role in conditioning. No reliable captive breeding protocol has been widely documented; most specimens in the trade are wild-caught. Cave dimensions of approximately 8–10 cm diameter and 30+ cm depth have been suggested, matching natural hollow-wood burrows.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Panaque nigrolineatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~43 cm), temperature (23–27°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness, native range (Orinoco and Amazon tributaries including Rio Negro), xylivorous diet requirements, driftwood necessity, filtration needs, conspecific aggression, and care level. Classification as one of very few wood-digesting vertebrates confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase — Panaque nigrolineatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (maximum reported ~43 cm TL), taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), native range (Colombia, Venezuela, Brazil — Orinoco and Amazon basins), temperature (22–28°C), and diet classification (herbivore/detritivore with wood-rasping noted).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pleco Care Guide
additional.notes: General pleco care; confirmed driftwood as essential requirement for wood-eating Panaque species, large tank requirements for Royal Pleco specifically, and heavy bioload management advice. Used for hobby-perspective tank size and filtration guidance.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Panaque nigrolineatus species profile
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (up to 43 cm), xylivory and hindgut cellulose-digesting microbes, mandatory driftwood supply, soft acidic water preference, Orinoco origin, conspecific aggression, and recommended tank size (minimum 450–500 L for a single adult).

additional.name: Planet Catfish — Panaque nigrolineatus (L190)
additional.notes: Specialist catfish database entry for L190. Confirmed L-number designation (L190), body colouration (dark brown/cream stripes, red eyes), max size (~43 cm), xyliphagous diet requirement, territorial behaviour, soft acidic water, and Orinoco basin origin. Used as corroborating specialist source.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is extremely rare, with no established protocol widely documented in the hobby. A very large species-only setup of at least 1,000 litres is considered necessary, with wood cave structures of approximately 8–10 cm diameter and 30 cm or more in depth to replicate the hollow-wood burrows used in the wild. Cave spawning with parental guarding of the eggs has been reported. The majority of Royal Plecos available in the trade are wild-caught.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 40–50cm; obligate wood-eater (xylivory — unique). Requires constant driftwood supply. Specialist large-pleco commitment, 500L+ tank.
